# Releasing

Omi v4 ships three independent release streams. Each one has its own tag pattern and its
own workflow; tagging one stream never builds the others.

| Stream | Tag pattern | Workflow | Runs on |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Firmware | `firmware-v*` | [`workflows/release-firmware.yml`](workflows/release-firmware.yml) | `ubuntu-24.04` inside `ghcr.io/nrfconnect/sdk-nrf-toolchain:v2.9.0` |
| Mobile | `mobile-v*` | [`workflows/release-mobile.yml`](workflows/release-mobile.yml) | `ubuntu-24.04` (Android) and `macos-15` (iOS) |
| Desktop | `desktop-v*` | [`workflows/release-desktop.yml`](workflows/release-desktop.yml) | `macos-15` |

`workflows/ci.yml` is unchanged and still runs the quality gates on every push and pull
request. The release workflows are additive and never run on plain pushes.

Every workflow also has a `workflow_dispatch` trigger with two inputs:

| Input | Type | Default on dispatch | Value on a tag push |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `version` | string | `dev` | the tag with its stream prefix stripped |
| `dry_run` | boolean | `true` | always `false` |

A dry run builds everything, checksums it and uploads it as workflow artifacts, but it
never creates a GitHub Release. Each workflow is split into a `bundle` job that always
runs and a `publish` job that is skipped entirely when `dry_run` is true. `publish` is the
only job that declares `permissions: contents: write`, so a dry run never has release-write
permission at all — the top-level default stays `contents: read`.

## Cutting a release

```sh
git tag firmware-v1.4.0 && git push origin firmware-v1.4.0
git tag mobile-v1.4.0   && git push origin mobile-v1.4.0
git tag desktop-v1.4.0  && git push origin desktop-v1.4.0
```

The version label used in artifact filenames is the tag with its stream prefix stripped,
so `firmware-v1.4.0` produces `omi-cv1-1.4.0-dfu_application.zip`.

Release notes are auto-generated by `softprops/action-gh-release@v2`
(`generate_release_notes: true`). Every release also carries a `SHA256SUMS.txt` covering
all of its artifacts.

## Dry-running a stream

Every stream can be exercised end to end without publishing anything:

1. *Actions → Release Firmware / Release Mobile / Release Desktop → Run workflow*.
2. Leave **Dry run** ticked (it defaults to on) and set **Version** to something like
   `dev` or `pr-123`.
3. Wait for the run. The `bundle` job uploads `<stream>-release-bundle`, containing every
   artifact plus `SHA256SUMS.txt`, to the run's Artifacts section. The `publish` job shows
   as skipped.

From the CLI:

```sh
gh workflow run release-firmware.yml -f version=dev -f dry_run=true
gh workflow run release-mobile.yml   -f version=dev -f dry_run=true
gh workflow run release-desktop.yml  -f version=dev -f dry_run=true
```

Setting `dry_run=false` on a manual run publishes a release against whatever ref the run
was dispatched from, so only do that deliberately. Tag pushes always run with
`dry_run=false`.

## Secrets

No secret is required to get a build out of any stream. Every signing step checks its
secrets first and, when they are missing, prints a `::notice::` and produces an unsigned
artifact instead of failing. Forks therefore still get usable downloads.

### Firmware — no secrets

The firmware stream needs nothing beyond the default `GITHUB_TOKEN`.

### Mobile

| Secret | Contents | Used for |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_BASE64` | Base64 of the release `.jks`/`.keystore` file (`base64 -i release.jks`) | Gradle `signingConfigs.release` |
| `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD` | Keystore password | Gradle `signingConfigs.release` |
| `ANDROID_KEY_ALIAS` | Alias of the signing key inside the keystore | Gradle `signingConfigs.release` |
| `ANDROID_KEY_PASSWORD` | Key password; falls back to `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD` when unset | Gradle `signingConfigs.release` |
| `IOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64` | Base64 of the exported iOS distribution certificate `.p12` | Building a signed `.ipa` |
| `IOS_CERTIFICATE_PASSWORD` | Password protecting that `.p12` | Building a signed `.ipa` |
| `IOS_PROVISIONING_PROFILE_BASE64` | Base64 of the `.mobileprovision` matching the app id and certificate | Building a signed `.ipa` |
| `IOS_EXPORT_METHOD` | `app-store`, `ad-hoc`, `enterprise` or `development`; defaults to `app-store` | Export options plist |
| `IOS_SIGNING_STYLE` | `automatic` (default) or `manual` | Export options plist |
| `APPLE_TEAM_ID` | 10-character Apple Developer team id | Export options plist |

`ANDROID_KEYSTORE_BASE64`, `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD` and `ANDROID_KEY_ALIAS` must all be
present or Gradle falls back to the debug signing config and the artifacts are named
`…-android-unsigned.apk` / `…-android-unsigned.aab`. All of `IOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64`,
`IOS_CERTIFICATE_PASSWORD`, `IOS_PROVISIONING_PROFILE_BASE64` and `APPLE_TEAM_ID` must be
present or the iOS job builds an unsigned `Runner.app` only.

#### Android signing

`app/android/app/build.gradle.kts` declares a real `signingConfigs.release`. It reads, in
order of precedence:

1. `app/android/key.properties` — `storeFile`, `storePassword`, `keyAlias`, `keyPassword`.
   A relative `storeFile` is resolved against `app/android/`. This file is gitignored;
   never commit it or the keystore it points at.
2. The environment — `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PATH`, `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD`,
   `ANDROID_KEY_ALIAS`, `ANDROID_KEY_PASSWORD` (the last defaults to
   `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PASSWORD`).

If neither source yields a keystore that actually exists on disk, the release build type
falls back to the debug signing config, so `flutter run --release` and fork builds keep
working without any credentials.

CI decodes `ANDROID_KEYSTORE_BASE64` into `$RUNNER_TEMP/release.keystore`, exports
`ANDROID_KEYSTORE_PATH` for the `flutter build apk` / `flutter build appbundle` steps, and
deletes the file afterwards. `flutter build` therefore emits an already-correctly-signed
APK and AAB; there is no `apksigner`/`jarsigner` re-signing pass any more. A verification
step runs `apksigner verify --print-certs` and **fails the job** if the APK came out
carrying `CN=Android Debug` despite the secrets being set, so a silent fallback to debug
signing cannot ship as a release.

#### iOS signing

The Xcode project (`app/ios/Runner.xcodeproj`) uses `CODE_SIGN_STYLE = Automatic` with
`DEVELOPMENT_TEAM` set, so the generated `ExportOptions.plist` now uses
`signingStyle: automatic` and the team id from `APPLE_TEAM_ID`. Set the `IOS_SIGNING_STYLE`
secret to `manual` only if you also switch the Xcode project to manual signing and provide
a matching `provisioningProfiles` mapping; the workflow rejects any other value.

### Desktop

| Secret | Contents | Used for |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `MACOS_CERTIFICATE_P12_BASE64` | Base64 of the exported *Developer ID Application* certificate `.p12` | Signing the app and the DMG |
| `MACOS_CERTIFICATE_PASSWORD` | Password protecting that `.p12` | Signing the app and the DMG |
| `MACOS_SIGNING_IDENTITY` | Full identity string, e.g. `Developer ID Application: Example Inc (LZ3NL5434Q)` | `codesign --sign` |
| `APPLE_ID` | Apple ID used for notarization | `xcrun notarytool submit` |
| `APPLE_APP_SPECIFIC_PASSWORD` | App-specific password for that Apple ID | `xcrun notarytool submit` |
| `APPLE_TEAM_ID` | 10-character Apple Developer team id | `xcrun notarytool submit` |

The repository signs with a development certificate locally. In CI, when the three
`MACOS_*` secrets are absent the app is ad-hoc signed (`codesign --sign -`) and the
artifacts are named `…-macos-unsigned.zip` / `…-macos-unsigned.dmg`. Gatekeeper will then
require a right-click → Open on first launch. Notarization additionally needs the three
Apple secrets; without them the notarize and staple steps are skipped.

Never commit a certificate, keystore or password. Add them under
*Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions*.

## How the firmware build matrix is discovered

The firmware matrix is **not** hardcoded in the workflow. A `discover` job runs
[`scripts/discover_firmware_targets.py`](scripts/discover_firmware_targets.py) against the
checkout and emits the matrix as JSON, which the `zephyr` job consumes via
`matrix: ${{ fromJSON(needs.discover.outputs.matrix) }}`. Resolution order:

1. **`firmware/README.md` is the source of truth.** The script parses every `west build`
   command it finds in the README — inside fenced code blocks or inline, across `\`
   continuations, honouring a preceding `cd <dir>` — and reads the board from `-b`/`--board`,
   the application directory from the positional source argument, `--sysbuild`, and any
   `-D…` cmake arguments (including everything after a `--` separator). The nearest
   preceding Markdown heading becomes the target id and device name. Documenting a target's
   exact build command in `firmware/README.md` is therefore all it takes to add or fix it.
2. **Otherwise the tree is walked.** Any directory under `firmware/` that contains a
   `CMakeLists.txt` calling `find_package(Zephyr …)` plus at least one `*.conf` is treated
   as an application. `sysbuild.conf`, `mcuboot.conf`, `b0.conf`, `hci_ipc.conf`,
   `ipc_radio.conf` and `empty_net_core.conf` are ignored, as are `boards/`, `dts/`,
   `modules/`, `tests/`, `samples/` and vendored `zephyr`/`nrf`/`bootloader` subtrees. Each
   remaining `prj_*.conf` becomes its own target (falling back to `prj.conf` when there are
   no variants). Sysbuild is enabled when the app has a `sysbuild.conf`/`sysbuild/` or its
   config sets `CONFIG_BOOTLOADER_MCUBOOT=y`.
3. **Boards come from `firmware/boards`.** Every `board.yml` is parsed for its name, socs
   and cpuclusters to build a qualified board string such as `omi/nrf5340/cpuapp`; legacy
   `*_defconfig` files contribute bare names. `-DBOARD_ROOT` is passed only when
   `firmware/boards` actually exists. A target's board is taken from the README command
   first, then by matching a discovered board name against the app path and config
   filename, then via the small declared `BOARD_HINTS` table in the script, then — if
   exactly one board exists in the tree — that one.
4. **A declared fallback matrix** (`FALLBACK_TARGETS` in the script) is used only when
   neither the README nor the tree yields anything.

Nothing here silently passes:

- No `firmware/` directory at all → the `discover` job fails with an explicit error, which
  skips `zephyr`, `bundle` and `publish`.
- A target whose board could not be resolved → its build job fails and prints every
  `board.yml` and `*_defconfig` under `firmware/boards`.
- A target whose `app_dir`/`conf` does not exist on disk (the fallback-matrix case) → its
  build job fails and lists the Zephyr application directories and `.conf` files it found.
- A build that produces none of the expected images → the collect step fails and dumps the
  build tree.

To see what the matrix would be for a given tree, run the script locally:

```sh
python3 .github/scripts/discover_firmware_targets.py --repo-root . --print-only
```

## Firmware artifacts and which device they flash

One `firmware-v*` tag builds every discovered device in the matrix, so a single release
covers all of the hardware. Artifact names are `<target-id>-<version>-<file>`, where
`<target-id>` is the slug of the README heading (or of the config file when the matrix was
derived from the tree).

Artifacts are located by **searching** the build directory rather than assuming sysbuild's
output layout: the collect step walks `build/<target-id>/` for `dfu_application.zip`,
`dfu_multi_image.bin`, `merged.hex`, `merged_domains.hex`, `app_update.bin`, `zephyr.hex`,
`zephyr.bin`, `zephyr.elf` and any `*.uf2`, skipping the `mcuboot/`, `b0/`, `b0n/`,
`hci_ipc/`, `ipc_radio/`, `empty_net_core/`, `_sysbuild/` and `CMakeFiles/` subtrees so the
application image wins over bootloader and network-core images of the same name.

The omiGlass job is separate and optional: it looks for a `platformio.ini` under
`firmware/` and skips with a notice when the ESP32 target has not been vendored.

Per device you get, depending on what the build produces:

- `…-dfu_application.zip` — MCUboot OTA package, for the CV1 pendant.
- `…-merged.hex` — full flash image for a wired J-Link programmer.
- `…-zephyr.hex`, `…-zephyr.bin` — application image only.
- `…-zephyr.uf2` — drag-and-drop image for the XIAO bootloader (DevKit targets).
- `…-app_update.bin` — MCUboot update image, when sysbuild emits one.

### Flashing over the air (recommended for the CV1 pendant)

1. Download `omi-cv1-<version>-dfu_application.zip` from the release. Do not unzip it.
2. Install **nRF Connect for Mobile** (Nordic Semiconductor) on iOS or Android.
3. Put the pendant in range and open nRF Connect → **Scan**, then connect to the device.
4. Open the **DFU** tab, choose **Select File**, pick the `.zip`, and start the update.
5. Leave the phone next to the pendant until the transfer reaches 100% and the device
   reboots on its own.

If the pendant does not advertise, charge it first — a low battery aborts DFU partway.

### Flashing with a J-Link / debug probe

```sh
nrfjprog --program omi-cv1-<version>-merged.hex --chiperase --verify --reset
```

Or with `west` if you have an nRF Connect SDK workspace:

```sh
west flash --hex-file omi-cv1-<version>-merged.hex
```

The merged hex includes MCUboot plus the application, so `--chiperase` is safe and gives
a known-good starting state. Flashing only `…-zephyr.hex` leaves the existing bootloader
in place.

### Flashing a DevKit (XIAO nRF52840 Sense)

Double-tap the reset button to enter the UF2 bootloader, then copy
`omi-devkit-…-zephyr.uf2` onto the mass-storage volume that appears. The board reboots
into the new firmware once the copy completes.

## Caching

- Flutter SDK and pub cache — `subosito/flutter-action@v2` with `cache: true`, plus
  `~/.pub-cache` in the `actions/cache@v4` entries.
- Gradle — `~/.gradle/caches` and `~/.gradle/wrapper`, keyed on the Gradle files.
- Cargo — `~/.cargo/registry`, `~/.cargo/git` and `app/native/hub/target`, keyed on
  `Cargo.toml`.
- CocoaPods — `~/Library/Caches/CocoaPods`, keyed on `Podfile.lock`.
- nRF Connect SDK — **not cached.** A full `/opt/ncs` west workspace is several gigabytes
  and would sit close to GitHub's 10 GB per-repository cache limit, where a save either
  fails or evicts every other cache in the repository. Every firmware job therefore does a
  cold `west init` + `west update`, narrowed as much as is safe:

  ```sh
  west config manifest.group-filter -- -babblesim,-ci,-optional,-debug
  west update --narrow -o=--depth=1
  ```

  That is roughly 5–15 minutes per matrix job. If you want the cache back, add an
  `actions/cache@v4` step around `${{ env.NCS_WORKSPACE }}` and prune `.git`, `doc/`,
  `tests/` and `samples/` before the save so the archive stays well under the limit —
  do not cache the workspace as-is.

## Toolchain resolution inside the NCS container

The `ghcr.io/nrfconnect/sdk-nrf-toolchain` image sets its environment from an entrypoint,
which GitHub Actions replaces, so `west`, `cmake` and the Zephyr SDK are frequently absent
from `PATH` in ordinary steps. [`scripts/setup-ncs-toolchain.sh`](scripts/setup-ncs-toolchain.sh)
resolves this deterministically, in order:

1. If `west` is already on `PATH`, nothing else is needed.
2. Otherwise `nrfutil toolchain-manager env --as-script` is sourced, the resulting
   environment is diffed against the pre-source environment, every genuinely new `PATH`
   entry is appended to `$GITHUB_PATH`, and `ZEPHYR_*`, `GNUARMEMB_*`, `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH`,
   `PYTHONPATH` and `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` are written to `$GITHUB_ENV`. Nothing is guessed from
   a hardcoded directory layout.
3. If `west` is still missing, an executable named `west` is searched for under
   `/opt/nordic`, `/opt`, `/usr/local` and `~/.local`, and its directory is prepended.
   `cmake`, `ninja` and `dtc` are located the same way if absent.
4. `ZEPHYR_SDK_INSTALL_DIR` is set to whichever directory actually contains an
   `sdk_version` file, and `ZEPHYR_TOOLCHAIN_VARIANT` defaults to `zephyr`.

If it still cannot find `west`, the script fails with `::error::` and prints exactly what
it searched, the contents of `/opt` and `/opt/nordic`, and the final `PATH` — so a changed
image layout produces a diagnosis rather than a mystery.

## Known rough edges

**None of these workflows has ever been executed.** They are YAML-valid and `actionlint`-clean,
the discovery script is unit-tested locally against synthetic firmware trees, and the shell
is `shellcheck`-clean, but no GitHub Actions run has confirmed any of it. Use a dry run
(above) before trusting a tag push. Specifically still unverified:

- Whether `nrfutil toolchain-manager env --as-script` exists and behaves as expected in
  `sdk-nrf-toolchain:v2.9.0`, and whether the fallback searches then find a usable toolchain.
- Whether the cold `west update` with the narrowed group filter still pulls every module
  the Omi applications need, and how long it actually takes.
- Whether the discovered board strings and cmake arguments match what the vendored
  `firmware/` tree really wants — `firmware/` is not on `main` yet, so the discovery
  script has only been exercised against synthetic trees.
- Whether the Gradle `signingConfigs.release` resolves in the Flutter Android build on CI
  (no JDK was available locally to evaluate the build script).
- Whether `signingStyle: automatic` exports cleanly for the configured provisioning
  profile, and whether notarization of the macOS app and DMG succeeds.
- macOS `flutter build macos --release` runs with the project's automatic signing and a
  `DEVELOPMENT_TEAM` set; if the build itself fails on a runner without a certificate,
  the fix is to make the Xcode project fall back to ad-hoc signing rather than to change
  this workflow.
